healthSafetyQuestionsDisclosed.description: IRA programs require answers to applicable Safety Testing questions be disclosed to the homeowner. Select “Yes” if disclosed, or “No” if not applicable.

yearBuilt.description: Enter the year the home or building was constructed. If there was a major addition made to the home, the "Year built" should be entered as the year the majority of the area that is heated and air conditioned was built.

conditionedArea.description: Enter the total area of the home that is heated or air conditioned. This may be different from the typical measure of the size of the home, especially if you have a heated garage or similar area of the home that receives heating or air conditioning. Make sure to include any space of the home that has heating or air conditioning, including conditioned basements (unless you mark the next field "Area Includes Basement" as No).

averageWallHeight.description:
Enter the average floor to ceiling wall height (internal measurement). This is used to calculate the volume of the home as well as the wall surface area.  
  
**Please note:** When we calculate the volume of the house, we add an additional foot of height for the basement rim joists (if there is a basement), and an additional foot of height for every story above one (if there are any).

houseLength.description:
Enter the length of the longest side of the smallest rectangle into which the home's footprint will fit.  
  
**Please note:** This is used only to calculate the surface area of the exterior walls, and has nothing to do with the conditioned square footage, volume, or any other calculations.  
  
This should give a fairly precise estimate of wall surface area in all circumstances except when the house is shaped like a U. If the home is shaped like a U, you'll need to override the total wall sqft in the Wall Details section with the additional surface area. See the knowledge base article on this for more information.

houseWidth.description:
Enter the width of the shortest side of the smallest rectangle into which the home's footprint will fit.  
  
**Please note:** This is used only to calculate the surface area of the exterior walls, and has nothing to do with the conditioned square footage, volume, or any other calculations.  
  
This should give a fairly precise estimate of wall surface area in all circumstances except when the house is shaped like a U. If the home is shaped like a U, you'll need to override the total wall sqft in the Wall Details section with the additional surface area. See the knowledge base article on this for more information.

floorsAboveGrade.description: Select the number of stories for the living area of this home, not counting any basement or walkout as a story. If this home is in a multi-story, multi-family complex, report only those stories in the living area of this particular home, which could likely be less than the total number of stories of the building.

numberOfOccupants.description:
Enter the number of people that live full-time in the home. If there are people that live in the home only part of the year (e.g., a student away at college or an adult that travels for business a significant amount each year) then for purposes of this audit count that individual as only 1/2 (0.5) of a person.  
  
This field directly affects human internal gains. If you don't set the bedrooms, then the occupants are used to estimate the bedrooms. Only the bedrooms field impact appliance usage levels. Occupants also impact the ASHRAE 62.2-2013 minimum ventilation.

typeOfHome.description:
Select the type of home that most closely resembles this home. Single-family Attached would be a duplex or a townhome with shared walls.  
  
**Please note:** that only multi-family units with independently metered fuel and electricity can be modeled with this software.

frontOfBuildingOrientation.description: Select the closest direction that the Length of the house as designated above faces (either of the opposite directions is fine). If the home is square, then choose the direction the main entry door of the home faces.

tuckUnderGarage.description:
Is there any conditioned living space over an unconditioned garage, cantilever, or frame floor that might need floor insulation?  
  
This data is used for the "Insulate Floors" recommendation. For cantilevers, adjust the total sqft on the refine screen or the details section.

garageSize.description:
Enter the number of cars that could fit in the the parking area of the garage. The software assumes an extra 100 sqft of storage and other space and each car takes up 200 sqft of space.  
  
This data is used for the "Insulate Floors" recommendation. For cantilevers, adjust the total sqft on the refine screen or the details section.

programmableThermostatInstalled.description: Is a programmable (setback) thermostat installed in the house? If it is installed, but not used, still check this box and adjust the thermostat settings accordingly. This button determines if a cost is added to the Programmable Thermostat recommendation. If you check Yes, then no cost will be added, but the software will still recommend ideal setpoints for the home for energy savings. If you check No, then a cost will be added to the recommendation and the ideal setpoints will also be shown in the recommendation.

heatingSetpointHigh.description:
Enter the heating thermostat temperature setting (°F) that is used most often when the home is occupied and used by people for normal daily activities. This is often the setting used when people are at home in the mornings before going to school or work and again when they return home from school and work in the afternoon or evening before they go to sleep for the night.  
  
**Note:** The goal of this is to determine the average temperature of the home for this time period and situation (cooling or heating). If the heating system just can't keep up and never meets the setpoint on cold days or similar for the cooling system, then adjust the setpoint to closer resemble the actual temperature of the home. Since this is hard to determine, you may enter a range of temperatures for each of the setpoints separated by a dash (no spaces). For example, you can set the Low temp setpoint for heating at 60-65 and the High temp setpoint at 68-71.

heatingSetpointHighImproved.description:
Enter the heating thermostat temperature setting (°F) that is used most often when the home is occupied and used by people for normal daily activities. This is often the setting used when people are at home in the mornings before going to school or work and again when they return home from school and work in the afternoon or evening before they go to sleep for the night.  
  
**Note:** The goal of this is to determine the average temperature of the home for this time period and situation (cooling or heating). If the heating system just can't keep up and never meets the setpoint on cold days or similar for the cooling system, then adjust the setpoint to closer resemble the actual temperature of the home. Since this is hard to determine, you may enter a range of temperatures for each of the setpoints separated by a dash (no spaces). For example, you can set the Low temp setpoint for heating at 60-65 and the High temp setpoint at 68-71.

heatingSetpointLow.description:
Enter the heating thermostat temperature setting (°F) that is used most often when the home is not occupied. This is often the setting used when people have left the home in the mornings to go to school or work and again when they have gone to sleep for the night.  
  
**Note:** The goal of this is to determine the average temperature of the home for this time period and situation (cooling or heating). If the heating system just can't keep up and never meets the setpoint on cold days or similar for the cooling system, then adjust the setpoint to closer resemble the actual temperature of the home. Since this is hard to determine, you may enter a range of temperatures for each of the setpoints separated by a dash (no spaces). For example, you can set the Low temp setpoint for heating at 60-65 and the High temp setpoint at 68-71.

totalNumberOfLightBulbs.description: Count the number of light bulbs in the home. This count assumes an average of 60 watts for each bulb. Remember to look in closets and other hidden areas for lights as well. In the Details section the Base and Improved numbers are calculated as a total of the number of bulbs for each side.

percentOfCeilingsShared.description: Select the % of heated or conditioned space (from a seperatly metered apartment or unit) above your apartment / condominium / single-family attached home? If this is a single family detached home, leave this blank or at 0.

percentOfFloorsShared.description: Select the % of heated or conditioned space (from a separately metered apartment or unit) below your apartment / condominium / single-family attached home? If this is a single family detached home, leave this blank or at 0.

skylightArea.description: Enter the area of any skylights (in square feet) or roof windows that are installed in the home within the heated or air conditioned space of the home.

blowerDoorReading.description:
The baseline airflow, in cubic feet per minute, through leaks in the building when there is a pressure difference between the building and ambient of 50 Pascals. This is the result of your blower door test if you perform one. If you do not enter a number here, the software will determine an appropriate number based on the age and size of the home.  
  
On the improved side, the default recommendation is a 25% reduction in the air leakage. You may of course override this to whatever number you feel you'll be able to achieve.

blowerDoorReadingImproved.description:
The baseline airflow, in cubic feet per minute, through leaks in the building when there is a pressure difference between the building and ambient of 50 Pascals. This is the result of your blower door test if you perform one. If you do not enter a number here, the software will determine an appropriate number based on the age and size of the home.  
  
On the improved side, the default recommendation is a 25% reduction in the air leakage. You may of course override this to whatever number you feel you'll be able to achieve.

This is the result of your blower door test if you perform one. If you do not enter a number here, the software will determine an appropriate number based on the age and size of the home.  
  
On the improved side, the default recommendation is a 25% reduction in the air leakage. You may of course override this to whatever number you feel you'll be able to achieve.

unmodeledBlowerDoorReadingImproved.description:

This is the result of your blower door test if you perform one. If you do not enter a number here, the software will determine an appropriate number based on the age and size of the home.  
  
On the improved side, the default recommendation is a 25% reduction in the air leakage. You may of course override this to whatever number you feel you'll be able to achieve.

equivalentNach.description: NACH is the number of times in one hour the entire volume of air inside the building leaks to the outside naturally. It is calculated from the Air Infiltration number in CFM50, the number of stories, the wind zone and type of shielding the house has.

equivalentNachImproved.description: NACH is the number of times in one hour the entire volume of air inside the building leaks to the outside naturally. It is calculated from the Air Infiltration number in CFM50, the number of stories, the wind zone and type of shielding the house has.

conditionedAirVolume.description: The conditioned air volume is calculated from the Conditioned Area and the Average Wall Height. This is really available for informational purposes and in most cases should not be edited.

effectiveLeakageArea.description: Effective (or Equivalent) Leakage Area in square inches. This is the area of a theoretical hole (with rounded edges) in the building envelope that would produce a leakage equivalent to that produced by the actual building at 4 Pascals of pressure.

effectiveLeakageAreaImproved.description: Effective (or Equivalent) Leakage Area in square inches. This is the area of a theoretical hole (with rounded edges) in the building envelope that would produce a leakage equivalent to that produced by the actual building at 4 Pascals of pressure.

equivalentAch50.description: ACH50 is the number of times in one hour the entire volume of air inside the building leaks to the outside when depressurized to 50 Pascals. This number is automatically calculated based on the house volume and the Air Infiltration number in CFM50.

equivalentAch50Improved.description: ACH50 is the number of times in one hour the entire volume of air inside the building leaks to the outside when depressurized to 50 Pascals. This number is automatically calculated based on the house volume and the Air Infiltration number in CFM50.

modeledBasementWallArea.description: This is the total wall area along the exposed perimeter (i.e. wall area eligible for insulation improvements). Warning: Changing the wall area will not effect the model. Wall area is only used in calculating improvement costs.

basementCavityInsulation.description:
Enter the total R-value of insulation installed (BASE) or to be installed (IMPROVED) in the framed cavity of the basement walls. If there is no framing, use Continuous Insulation below to specify insulation types such as fiberglass drape or rigid insulation.  
  
**Note:**  
Edit Base fields in the Details section only if you're confident of the actual value that is entered. The modeling engine utilizes utility data and input form data to determine the "effective" values on the Base side.  
  
Edit Improved fields in the Details section with the details of what you recommend be installed in the house. These values are only used if the item is Recommended. Default values are supplied by the modeling engine, but can be overridden.

basementCavityInsulationImproved.description:

**Note:**  
Edit Base fields in the Details section only if you're confident of the actual value that is entered. The modeling engine utilizes utility data and input form data to determine the "effective" values on the Base side.

This field is not editable since it is calculated from the other fields.

modeledCrawlWallArea.description:
This field will be automatically calculated based on the data you entered in the input form for House Length and House Width. This is the total wall area along the exposed perimeter (i.e. wall area eligible for insulation improvements).

modeledCrawlFloorArea.description: This field will be automatically calculated based on the data you entered in the input form for Conditioned Area, Number of Stories, and Foundation Makeup.

crawlspaceType.description:
Choose the type of venting and conditioning in the crawlspace. Your choice will enable or disable the Wall and Floor Cavity Insulation fields in the Crawlspace Details section depending on the appropriate situation.  
  
**Unvented - Unconditioned Crawl**: This unconditioned crawlspace scenario is where the building floor above the crawl space is the thermal boundary to the home. If there were insulation, it would be applied applied to the building floor (crawlspace ceiling). This crawlspace does not have direct vents to the outside air.  
  
**Vented - Year Round**: This is an unconditioned crawlspace where the building floor above the crawl space is the thermal boundary to the home. If there were insulation, it would be applied to the building floor (crawlspace ceiling). This crawlspace has direct vents to the outside air, and those vents remain open all year long.  
  
**Vented - Summer Only**: This is an unconditioned crawlspace where the building floor above the crawl space is the thermal boundary to the home. If there were insulation, it would be applied to the building floor (crawlspace ceiling). This crawlspace has direct vents to the outside air, and those vents remain open only during summer months.  
  
**Conditioned Crawl**: This is a conditioned crawlspace where the foundation walls and ground/slab of the crawl space are the thermal boundary. If there is insulation, it would be applied to the foundation wall and rim joists. In this scenario, there is no venting to the outside and the crawlspace floor is covered with a vapor barrier on top of the dirt floor. Building code often requires adding a jump vent to the main conditioned space in the house or ducting the furnace and/or A/C into the crawlspace as well. If the crawl does not have a jump vent or supply ducts, you will likely get a slight overestimate of energy use. The model assumes that the crawl temperature is a mixture of 20% ground temp and 80% indoor temp. Note: This field also triggers the Rim Joist options for the Crawlspace. You must set this field to Conditioned Crawl to be able to specify the insulation of the Rim Joists in the Crawlspace.

crawlspaceTypeImproved.description:
Choose the type of venting and conditioning in the crawlspace. Your choice will enable or disable the Wall and Floor Cavity Insulation fields in the Crawlspace Details section depending on the appropriate situation.  
  
**Unvented - Unconditioned Crawl**: This unconditioned crawlspace scenario is where the building floor above the crawl space is the thermal boundary to the home. If there were insulation, it would be applied applied to the building floor (crawlspace ceiling). This crawlspace does not have direct vents to the outside air.  
  
**Vented - Year Round**: This is an unconditioned crawlspace where the building floor above the crawl space is the thermal boundary to the home. If there were insulation, it would be applied to the building floor (crawlspace ceiling). This crawlspace has direct vents to the outside air, and those vents remain open all year long.  
  
**Vented - Summer Only**: This is an unconditioned crawlspace where the building floor above the crawl space is the thermal boundary to the home. If there were insulation, it would be applied to the building floor (crawlspace ceiling). This crawlspace has direct vents to the outside air, and those vents remain open only during summer months.  
  
**Conditioned Crawl**: This is a conditioned crawlspace where the foundation walls and ground/slab of the crawl space are the thermal boundary. If there is insulation, it would be applied to the foundation wall and rim joists. In this scenario, there is no venting to the outside and the crawlspace floor is covered with a vapor barrier on top of the dirt floor. Building code often requires adding a jump vent to the main conditioned space in the house or ducting the furnace and/or A/C into the crawlspace as well. If the crawl does not have a jump vent or supply ducts, you will likely get a slight overestimate of energy use. The model assumes that the crawl temperature is a mixture of 20% ground temp and 80% indoor temp. Note: This field also triggers the Rim Joist options for the Crawlspace. You must set this field to Conditioned Crawl to be able to specify the insulation of the Rim Joists in the Crawlspace.

clothesWasherType.description: Enter the type of Clothes Washer, if any, that is installed in the house.

clothesWasherMef.description: Enter the Clothes Washer's Integrated Modified Energy Factor (IMEF) if available. The higher the IMEF, the more efficient the dishwasher. Search our knowledge base for "Appliances Product Finder" for more information.

clothesWasherMefImproved.description: Enter the Clothes Washer's Integrated Modified Energy Factor (IMEF) if available. The higher the IMEF, the more efficient the dishwasher. Search our knowledge base for "Appliances Product Finder" for more information.

clothesWasherEnergyUsageImproved.description:
As an alternative to entering the IMEF, you may enter the rated energy consumption in kWh / year. You must also enter the rated water use in gallons per year.  
  
Previously the API incorrectly returned this improved value under clothesWasherEnergyUsage. This value can still be found there for backwards compatibility, however the correct key for this value is clothesWasherEnergyUsageImproved. Note that there never was a base equivalent for this value, only an improved one.

clothesWasherWaterUsageImproved.description:
As an alternative to entering the IMEF, you may enter the rated water use in gallons per year. You must also enter the rated energy consumption in kWh / year above.  
  
Previously the API incorrectly returned this improved value under clothesWasherWaterUsage. This value can still be found there for backwards compatibility, however the correct key for this value is clothesWasherWaterUsageImproved. Note that there never was a base equivalent for this value, only an improved one.

dishwasherEnergyStar.description: Choose Yes if the Dishwasher is an ENERGY STAR model.

dishwasherEnergyStarImproved.description: Choose Yes if the Dishwasher is an ENERGY STAR model.

dishwasherEnergyFactor.description: Enter the Dishwasher's Energy Factor (EF) if available. The higher the EF, the more efficient the dishwasher. Search our knowledge base for "Appliances Product Finder" for more information.

dishwasherEnergyFactorImproved.description: Enter the Dishwasher's Energy Factor (EF) if available. The higher the EF, the more efficient the dishwasher. Search our knowledge base for "Appliances Product Finder" for more information.

percentCflOrLed.description: Use this field to override the number of CFLs or LEDs that exist in the home before the retrofit.

percentCflOrLedImproved.description: Use this field to override the number of CFLs or LEDs that exist in the home before the retrofit.

windZone.description: Select the Wind Zone from the map.

shielding.description:
Shielding type used to determine NACH N-factor:  
  

*   **Well-Shielded** - urban areas with high buildings or sheltered areas
*   **Normal** - surrounded by trees or other buildings
*   **Exposed** - dwelling is not surrounded by any objects

nFactor.description: Correlation Factor developed by Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory for the calculation of NACH.

poolPumpType.description: Select the Pump Type from Single Speed, Two Speed, or Variable Speed (or No Improvement or None). Enter the HP (or leave blank for the default) for a Single Speed or Two Speed pump, or Turnover for a variable speed pump. Wattage of variable speed pumps depends on the speed the pump is running, and the speed will depend on the turnover, so electrical usage is determined by turnover (number of times all the water in the pool circulates through the filter in a day). The Variable Speed Pump analysis includes an optimization routine that calculates the minimal electrical usage for a variable speed pump for the selected turnover. Therefore proper setup of the pump is required to achieve these results.

poolPumpTypeImproved.description: Select the Pump Type from Single Speed, Two Speed, or Variable Speed (or No Improvement or None). Enter the HP (or leave blank for the default) for a Single Speed or Two Speed pump, or Turnover for a variable speed pump. Wattage of variable speed pumps depends on the speed the pump is running, and the speed will depend on the turnover, so electrical usage is determined by turnover (number of times all the water in the pool circulates through the filter in a day). The Variable Speed Pump analysis includes an optimization routine that calculates the minimal electrical usage for a variable speed pump for the selected turnover. Therefore proper setup of the pump is required to achieve these results.

basementHeating.description:
Please describe the heating situation in the basement.  
  
**Intentional:**  

*   Basement receives heat in the same manner as the rest of the house.

**Intentional w/ continuous circulation:**  

*   Basement air is continuously exchanged with upper floors, equalizing temperatures.

**Incidental-Desired (e.g. leaky ducts):**  

*   Basement is semi-conditioned by presence of HVAC equipment.
*   Semi-conditioning is desired and measures will not be taken to eliminate it.
*   Thermal boundary for model will be located at basement walls and floor.

**None or Undesired Incidental:**  

*   Basement is not intentionally conditioned.
*   Incidental conditioning is undesired and considered wasted energy.
*   Thermal boundary for model will be the building floor above the basement.

basementHeatingImproved.description:
Please describe the heating situation in the basement.  
  
**Intentional:**  

*   Basement receives heat in the same manner as the rest of the house.

**Intentional w/ continuous circulation:**

**Incidental-Desired (e.g. leaky ducts):**  

*   Basement is semi-conditioned by presence of HVAC equipment.
*   Semi-conditioning is desired and measures will not be taken to eliminate it.
*   Thermal boundary for model will be located at basement walls and floor.

**None or Undesired Incidental:**  

*   Basement is not intentionally conditioned.
*   Incidental conditioning is undesired and considered wasted energy.
*   Thermal boundary for model will be the building floor above the basement.

basementCooling.description:
Please describe the cooling situation in the basement.  
  
**Intentional:**  

*   Basement receives cooling in the same manner as the rest of the house.

**Intentional w/ continuous circulation:**  

*   Basement air is continuously exchanged with upper floors, equalizing temperatures.

**Incidental-Desired (e.g. leaky ducts):**  

*   Basement is semi-conditioned by presence of HVAC equipment.
*   Semi-conditioning is desired and measures will not be taken to eliminate it.
*   Thermal boundary for model will be located at basement walls and floor.

**None or Undesired Incidental:**  

*   Basement is not intentionally conditioned.
*   Incidental conditioning is undesired and considered wasted energy.
*   Thermal boundary for model will be the building floor above the basement.

**Note:** If you choose "None or Undesired Incidental", then be sure to NOT include the basement square footage in the conditioned area of the home under the Building inputs.

crawlspaceRimJoistLength.description:
This field will be automatically calculated based on the data you entered in the input form for House Length and House Width. This is the total length of the exposed perimeter of the rim joist for the crawlspace.  
  
**Note**: This field is always displayed, regardless if the Rim Joist is actually insulated in the Crawlspace. In order for the Rim Joist to be counted in the modeling and be designated in the HPXML output, the Crawlspace Type must be set as Conditioned Crawl. Otherwise, the Rim Joist is counted as part of the Frame Floor (the crawlspace ceiling).

crawlspaceRimJoistTreatment.description:
Choose whether or not the Rim Joist is treated in the same was as the Crawlspace Wall or separately.  
  
**Note**: This field is triggered by the Crawlspace Type. In order for the Rim Joist to be counted in the modeling and be designated in the HPXML output, the Crawlspace Type must be set as Conditioned Crawl. Otherwise, the Rim Joist is counted as part of the Frame Floor (the crawlspace ceiling).

crawlspaceRimJoistTreatmentImproved.description:
Choose whether or not the Rim Joist is treated in the same was as the Crawlspace Wall or separately.  
  
**Note**: This field is triggered by the Crawlspace Type. In order for the Rim Joist to be counted in the modeling and be designated in the HPXML output, the Crawlspace Type must be set as Conditioned Crawl. Otherwise, the Rim Joist is counted as part of the Frame Floor (the crawlspace ceiling).

crawlspaceRimJoistInsulation.description:
Enter the R-value of the insulation for the Base or Improved Rim Joist in the Crawlspace.  
  
**Note**: This field is triggered by the Crawlspace Type. In order for the Rim Joist to be counted in the modeling and be designated in the HPXML output, the Crawlspace Type must be set as Conditioned Crawl. Otherwise, the Rim Joist is counted as part of the Frame Floor (the crawlspace ceiling).

crawlspaceRimJoistInsulationImproved.description:
Enter the R-value of the insulation for the Base or Improved Rim Joist in the Crawlspace.  
  
**Note**: This field is triggered by the Crawlspace Type. In order for the Rim Joist to be counted in the modeling and be designated in the HPXML output, the Crawlspace Type must be set as Conditioned Crawl. Otherwise, the Rim Joist is counted as part of the Frame Floor (the crawlspace ceiling).

crawlRimJoistAssemblyRValue.description: This is the total assembly R-value for the crawlspace im joists. We take the data from the Crawlspace Rim Joist Insulation field and add factors for wood framing, air film, sheathing, etc. to calculate the overall assembly R-value. This field is not editable since it is calculated from the other fields.

ashraeMinimumCfm50Improved.description:
Minimum infiltration in CFM50 below which ASHRAE 62.2-2013 recommends mechanical ventilation.  
  
N/A indicates that either the local ventilation inputs are not complete or the home was built after 2009. Homes built after 2009 are not eligible for infiltration credit towards whole house ventilation.  
  
Previously the API incorrectly returned this improved value under ashraeMinimumCfm50. This value can still be found there for backwards compatibility, however the correct key for this value is ashraeMinimumCfm50Improved. Note that there never was a base equivalent for this value, only an improved one.

dishwasherEnergyUsageImproved.description:
As an alternative to entering the EF, you may enter the rated energy consumption in kWh / year. You must also enter the rated water use in gallons per year.  
  
Previously the API incorrectly returned this improved value under dishwasherEnergyUsage. This value can still be found there for backwards compatibility, however the correct key for this value is dishwasherEnergyUsageImproved. Note that there never was a base equivalent for this value, only an improved one.

dishwasherWaterUsageImproved.description:
As an alternative to entering the EF, you may enter the rated water use in gallons per cycle. You must also enter the rated energy consumption in kWh / year above.  
  
Previously the API incorrectly returned this improved value under dishwasherWaterUsage. This value can still be found there for backwards compatibility, however the correct key for this value is dishwasherWaterUsageImproved. Note that there never was a base equivalent for this value, only an improved one.

basementCeilingCavityInsulation.description:
Enter the total R-value of insulation installed (BASE) or to be installed (IMPROVED) in the framed cavity of the basement ceiling. This field is active only if you are treating the basement as an unconditioned space and have the Basement Heating or Basement Cooling fields set to "None or Undesired Incidnetal".  
  
**Note:**  
Edit Base fields in the Details section only if you're confident of the actual value that is entered. The modeling engine utilizes utility data and input form data to determine the "effective" values on the Base side.  
  
Edit Improved fields in the Details section with the details of what you recommend be installed in the house. These values are only used if the item is Recommended. Default values are supplied by the modeling engine, but can be overridden.

basementCeilingCavityInsulationImproved.description:
Enter the total R-value of insulation installed (BASE) or to be installed (IMPROVED) in the framed cavity of the basement ceiling. This field is active only if you are treating the basement as an unconditioned space and have the Basement Heating or Basement Cooling fields set to "None or Undesired Incidnetal".  
  
**Note:**  
Edit Base fields in the Details section only if you're confident of the actual value that is entered. The modeling engine utilizes utility data and input form data to determine the "effective" values on the Base side.  
  
Edit Improved fields in the Details section with the details of what you recommend be installed in the house. These values are only used if the item is Recommended. Default values are supplied by the modeling engine, but can be overridden.

basementCeilingCavityInsulationTypeImpro.description: Select the type of insulation that is installed in the crawlspace ceiling cavity. If more than one type of insulation is installed, select the type that covers that largest area.

basementCeilingContinuousInsulation.description:
Enter the total R-value of continuous insulation installed or to be installed on the basement ceiling. Continuous insulation is any insulation like spray foam or rigid foam that is continuous and consistent in R-value across studs, joists, or any framing member.  
  
**Note:**  
Edit Base fields in the Details section only if you're confident of the actual value that is entered. The modeling engine utilizes utility data and input form data to determine the "effective" values on the Base side.  
  
Edit Improved fields in the Details section with the details of what you recommend be installed in the house. These values are only used if the item is Recommended. Default values are supplied by the modeling engine, but can be overridden.

basementCeilingContinuousInsulationImpro.description:
Enter the total R-value of continuous insulation installed or to be installed on the basement ceiling. Continuous insulation is any insulation like spray foam or rigid foam that is continuous and consistent in R-value across studs, joists, or any framing member.  
  
**Note:**  
Edit Base fields in the Details section only if you're confident of the actual value that is entered. The modeling engine utilizes utility data and input form data to determine the "effective" values on the Base side.  
  
Edit Improved fields in the Details section with the details of what you recommend be installed in the house. These values are only used if the item is Recommended. Default values are supplied by the modeling engine, but can be overridden.

basementCeilingContinuousInsulationType.description: Select the type of continuous insulation that is installed in the basement ceiling. If more than one type of insulation is installed, select the type that covers that largest area.

basementCeilingContinuousInsulationTypeI.description: Select the type of continuous insulation that is installed in the basement ceiling. If more than one type of insulation is installed, select the type that covers that largest area.

basementCeilingAssemblyRValue.description: This is the total assembly R-value for the entire basement ceiling system. We take the data from the Basement Ceiling Cavity R-value field and the Basement Ceiling Continuous Insulation R-value field add factors for wood framing, carpet, air film, sheathing, etc. to calculate the overall assembly R-value. This field is not editable since it is calculated from the other fields.

basementCeilingAssemblyRValueImproved.description: This is the total assembly R-value for the entire basement ceiling system. We take the data from the Basement Ceiling Cavity R-value field and the Basement Ceiling Continuous Insulation R-value field add factors for wood framing, carpet, air film, sheathing, etc. to calculate the overall assembly R-value. This field is not editable since it is calculated from the other fields.

ventilationType.description: Select the type of mechanical ventilation that is installed in the home such as an HRV or exhaust fan hardwired to a timer that runs for a fixed amount of time throughout the day. Do not enter spot ventilation systems here such as bath or kitchen fans that are controlled by the occupant.

ventilationTypeImproved.description: Select the type of mechanical ventilation that is installed in the home such as an HRV or exhaust fan hardwired to a timer that runs for a fixed amount of time throughout the day. Do not enter spot ventilation systems here such as bath or kitchen fans that are controlled by the occupant.

ventilationSchedule.description: Choose whether the mechanical ventilation system runs continuously or intermittently throughout the day

ventilationScheduleImproved.description: Choose whether the mechanical ventilation system runs continuously or intermittently throughout the day

ventilationCycleFrequency.description: How often does the ventilation system turn on in hours? For instance, if the ventilation system runs for 15 minutes every 4 hours, enter 4 in this field and 0.25 in the Ventilation Cycle Duration field. This field is hidden if the ventilation system runs continuously.

ventilationCycleFrequencyImproved.description: How often does the ventilation system turn on in hours? For instance, if the ventilation system runs for 15 minutes every 4 hours, enter 4 in this field and 0.25 in the Ventilation Cycle Duration field. This field is hidden if the ventilation system runs continuously.

ventilationCycleDuration.description: When the ventilation system turns on, how long does it run for each time? For instance, if the ventilation system runs for 15 minutes every 4 hours, enter 4 in the Ventilation Cycle Frequency field and 0.25 in this field. This field is hidden if the ventilation system runs continuously.

ventilationCycleDurationImproved.description: When the ventilation system turns on, how long does it run for each time? For instance, if the ventilation system runs for 15 minutes every 4 hours, enter 4 in the Ventilation Cycle Frequency field and 0.25 in this field. This field is hidden if the ventilation system runs continuously.

ventilationFlowRate.description: What is the flow rate of the mechanical ventilation fan in CFM?

ventilationFlowRateImproved.description: What is the flow rate of the mechanical ventilation fan in CFM?

ventilationSensibleRecoveryEfficiency.description: Enter the Max Rated Sensible Recovery Efficiency as listed in the Home Ventilating Industry (HVI) Product Directory. https://www.hvi.org/hvi-certified-products-directory. This field is hidden unless Energy Recovery Ventilator or Heat Recovery Ventilator is chosen as the Ventilation Type.

ventilationSensibleRecoveryEfficiencyImp.description: Enter the Max Rated Sensible Recovery Efficiency as listed in the Home Ventilating Industry (HVI) Product Directory. https://www.hvi.org/hvi-certified-products-directory. This field is hidden unless Energy Recovery Ventilator or Heat Recovery Ventilator is chosen as the Ventilation Type.

ventilationTotalRecoveryEfficiency.description: Enter the Latent Recovery / Moisture Transfer (LRMT) as listed in the Home Ventilating Industry (HVI) Product Directory. You may have to click on the "More Details" button in the directory to find this column in the Energy Ratings section. https://www.hvi.org/hvi-certified-products-directory. This field is hidden unless Energy Recovery Ventilator is chosen as the Ventilation Type.

ventilationTotalRecoveryEfficiencyImprov.description: Enter the Latent Recovery / Moisture Transfer (LRMT) as listed in the Home Ventilating Industry (HVI) Product Directory. You may have to click on the "More Details" button in the directory to find this column in the Energy Ratings section. https://www.hvi.org/hvi-certified-products-directory. This field is hidden unless Energy Recovery Ventilator is chosen as the Ventilation Type.

ventilationFanPower.description: Enter the Input Power or the Power Consumed @ Max Rated SRE as listed in the Home Ventilating Industry (HVI) Product Directory: https://www.hvi.org/hvi-certified-products-directory.

ventilationFanPowerImproved.description: Enter the Input Power or the Power Consumed @ Max Rated SRE as listed in the Home Ventilating Industry (HVI) Product Directory: https://www.hvi.org/hvi-certified-products-directory.

wholeHomeIsProfessionallyAirSealed.description: Choose Yes if Whole home is professionally air sealed.

hesBaseScore.description: The Home Energy Score for the home.

hesLabelNumber.description: The identification number for this specific home energy score.

hesHescoreVersion.description: The Home Energy Score version for this score.

hesSourceEnergyTotalBase.description: The total source energy calculated by the Home Energy Score.

hesSourceEnergyAssetBase.description: The source energy calculated by the Home Energy Score.

hesBuildingId.description: The building id generated by the Home Energy Score.

hesPercentEnergySaved.description: The difference between current Home Energy Score Source Energy Total base and the initial Source Energy Total Base

uuid.description: This is a universally unique ID for this item (UUID).

This is a universally unique ID for this item (UUID).

category.description:
Field options:  
directInstalls → Direct Installs  
rebatesIncentives → Rebates & Incentives  
costs → Costs

uuid.description: This is a universally unique ID for this item (UUID).

paymentInfo.description: This is the payment information that appears in the work order.

legalInfo.description: This is the legal information that appears in the work order.

discountPercentage.description: Discount percentage applied on subtotal (pre-tax). The discount can either be \`discountPercentage\` or \`discountDollar\`, but not both. You can calculate the discount percentage based on the subtotal of the work order.

discountDollar.description: Flat discount dollar amount. The discount can either be \`discountPercentage\` or \`discountDollar\`, but not both. In the API, this number is repeated in the discoutnAmount value if the discount is a flat dollar reduction.

stateTax.description: State tax rate expressed as a percentage

countyTax.description: County tax rate expressed as a percentage

cityTax.description: City tax rate expressed as a percentage

amountPaid.description: This is the amount already paid by the customer. The remaining balance can be found by taking the total job costs and subtracting the amount paid.

subTotal.description: Subtotal of work order before discount and taxes.

discountAmount.description: Returns the discount amount wether a percentage or a flat dollar amount was applied. This may be a duplicate of discountDollar if discount was provided as a flat dollar discount instead of a percentage discount.

applicableTax.description: Total of all taxes on subtotal after discount.

total.description: This is the grand total of the work order after applicable discount and taxes.

billEntryType.description:
Choose a method of utility bill entry. Always use detailed bills if you have 13 months of meter read dates with associated fuel and electricity usage. Detailed bills will provide more accurate energy savings estimates. If you can get monthly bills that cover the primary heating and cooling season, then choose detailed bills.  
  
Simple Bills can be used if the home has not been occupied by the current occupants for more than 13 months or you don't have access to detailed bills for the current occupants. You can typically get the high and low bills for gas and electric for any address by calling the utility's customer service number and requesting it (even without permission of the tenants at the time).

electricUtilityProviderName.description: Enter the name of the electric utility provider. This is an optional field for most users, but could be required for certain efficiency programs.

electricAccountNumber.description: Enter the utility account number for the meter that these bills correspond to. This is an optional field for most users, but could be required for certain efficiency programs.

highestMonthlySummerElectricBill.description:
Enter the amount of the highest monthly summertime electricity bill for the home during the past 12 months. Summertime is typically those months when many buildings would use air conditioning to cool the building.  
  
If the home is on a budget plan where the monthly electricity bills are all the same, the highest bill can be estimated by looking for the summer month with the highest electricity usage and then multiplying that amount by the electricity rate being charged.  
  
**Notes:**  
Applicable to bills of `Simple` entry type

lowestMonthlyElectricBill.description:
Enter the amount of the lowest of all the monthly electricity bills for the home during the past 12 months. If the home is on a budget plan where the monthly electricity bills are all the same, the lowest bill can be estimated by looking for the month with the lowest electricity usage and then multiplying that amount by the electricity rate being charged.  
  
**Notes:**  
Applicable to bills of `Simple` entry type

electricBillUnits.description:
Enter the units used for the electric bills. This is a required field if you're going to use Detailed Bills. For best results, utilize the kWh option instead of dollars for more accurate representations of the home.  
  
**Notes:**  
Applicable to bills of `Detailed` entry type

startElectricDate1.description:
**Start with the oldest bill first.**  
  
It's very important to enter meter read dates as opposed to billing dates.  

*   If you have 13 months of utility bills, enter the meter read date of the oldest of the 13 bills.
*   If you only have 12 months of utility bills, look at the oldest bill. Search for a previous meter read date. Enter that here.  
    If you can't find a previous meter read date, then search "number of days in billing period". Enter a date that's this number of days prior to the meter read date you're looking at.

_See the knowledge base for more information on entering utility bills._  
  
**Notes:**  
Applicable to bills of `Detailed` entry type

endElectricDate1.description:
*   If you only have 12 months of utility bills, enter the meter read date for **the oldest bill** you have.
*   If you have 13 months of bills, enter the meter read date and usage for the **2nd oldest bill**.
*   Then enter the energy usage from the corresponding date to the left. Be sure that this usage is for the time period between the End Bill Date to the left and the previous date in this list.

  
  
**Notes:**  
Applicable to bills of `Detailed` entry type

endElectricBill1.description:
**Notes:**  
Applicable to bills of `Detailed` entry type

endElectricDate2.description:
*   Fill out as many of these meter read dates as you have available.
*   This detailed input section can be used with any amount of available data.
*   Enter zero for missing or abnormal bills. The utility analysis will treat an entry of zero as missing and will do its best to compensate for missing bills.
*   To include a bill with actual zero usage, enter 0.01.
*   Be sure that you're entering the usage for the corresponding measurement that you designated in the Bill Units above (ie. dollars or kWh).

  
  
**Notes:**  
Applicable to bills of `Detailed` entry type

endElectricBill2.description:
**Notes:**  
Applicable to bills of `Detailed` entry type

primaryHeatingFuelType.description:
This is the primary fuel source. It is used to set prices and energy content.  
  
**Notes:**  
Applicable to bills of `Detailed` entry type

fuelUtilityProviderName.description: Enter the name of the fuel utility provider. This is an optional field for most users, but could be required for certain efficiency programs.

fuelAccountNumber.description: Enter the utility account number for the meter that the fuel bills correspond to. This is an optional field for most users, but could be required for certain efficiency programs.

additionalFuelUtilityProviderName.description: Enter the name of an additional fuel utility provider if applicable. This is an optional field for most users, but could be required for certain efficiency programs.

additionalFuelAccountNumber.description: Enter the utility additional account number for the meter that the fuel bills correspond to. This is an optional field for most users, but could be required for certain efficiency programs.

highestMonthlyWinterElectricBill.description:
Enter the amount of the highest monthly winter electric bill for the home during the past 12 months.  
  
If the home is on a budget plan where the monthly electricity bills are all the same, the highest bill can be estimated by looking for the winter month with the highest electricity usage and then multiplying that amount by the electricity rate being charged.  
  
**Notes:**  
Applicable to bills of `Simple` entry type

highestMonthlyWinterNaturalGasBill.description:
Enter the amount of the highest monthly winter gas bill for the home during the past 12 months.  
  
If the home is on a budget plan where the monthly gas bills are all the same, the highest bill can be estimated by looking for the winter month with the highest gas usage and then multiplying that amount by the gas rate being charged. If the home is heated by more than one type of fuel, use the bill from the type which provides the heat for the largest area of the home.  
  
**Notes:**  
Applicable to bills of `Simple` entry type

lowestMonthlyNaturalGasBill.description:
This is typically in the summer time when you're only using fuel for hot water and cooking.  
  
If the home uses more than one type, use the bill from the type which is used the most within the home.  
  
**Notes:**  
Applicable to bills of `Simple` entry type

simpleFuelUnits.description:
**Notes:**  
Applicable to bills of `Simple` entry type

totalSimpleFuelUsedInLast12Months.description:
Take a look at your delivery receipts and add up the total gallons delivered over the past 12 months. If you don't have information on the gallons, you can look at bank statements, etc. to total up how much you've spent on deliveries over the past 12 months.  
  
**Notes:**  
Applicable to bills of `Simple` entry type

fuelBillUnits.description:
The units used for the fuel bills. This is a required field if you're going to use Detailed Bills. For best results, utilize the kWh option instead of dollars for more accurate representations of the home.  
  
**Notes:**  
Applicable to bills of `Detailed` entry type

detailedElectricDataSource.description: Choose a data source of electric utility bill entry.

detailedFuelDataSource.description: Choose a data source of primary fuel utility bill entry.

utilityPriceNaturalGas.description: Enter a your own price for Natural Gas to override the software's existing regional defaults from the EIA.

utilityPricePropane.description: Enter a your own price for Propane to override the software's existing regional defaults from the EIA.

utilityPriceFuelOil.description: Enter a your own price for Fuel Oil to override the software's existing regional defaults from the EIA.

utilityPriceElectricity.description: Enter a your own price for Electricity to override the software's existing regional defaults from the EIA.

utilityPriceWood.description: Enter a your own price for wood. We do not have default costs for wood, therefore this must be filled out unless the wood supply is free.

utilityPricePellets.description: Enter a your own price for wood pellets. We do not have default costs for wood pellets, therefore this must be filled out if pellets are chosen in the heating system fuel options.

utilityPriceCoal.description: Enter your own price for coal. We do not have default costs for coal, therefore this must be filled out if coal is chosen in heating fuel options.

utilityPriceKerosene.description: Enter your own price for kerosene. We do not have default costs for kerosene, therefore this must be filled out if kerosene is chosen in heating fuel options.

fuelRatePlan.description: Enter the name of the utility fuel rate plan. For gas, the same rate plan is used for both Base and Improved; separate Improved Fuel Rate Plans are not supported.

uuid.description: This is a universally unique ID for this item (UUID).

altId.description: An optional ID to identify the direct install.

installDate.description: The date of installation for these measures.

discountPercentage.description: Percentage of discount. This is an either/or. The discount is either percentage or dollars but not both

discountDollar.description: Dollar amount of discount. This is an either/or. The discount is either percentage or dollars but not both

electricUtilityCopay.description: Amount of money that will be paid by the electric utility company. This is subtracted after taxes before determining the total due by the homeowner.

gasUtilityCopay.description: Amount of money that will be paid by the gas utility company. This is subtracted after taxes before determining the total due by the homeowner.

waterUtilityCopay.description: Amount of money that will be paid by the water utility company. This is subtracted after taxes before determining the total due by the homeowner.

programCopay.description: Amount of money that will be paid by the program. This is subtracted after taxes before determining the total due by the homeowner.

otherCopay.description: Amount of money that will be paid by the utility company. This is subtracted after taxes before determining the total due by the homeowner.

laborIsTaxable.description:
Is labor taxable? Defaults to not taxable. This value is set at the program level.  
Field options:  
1 → Yes  
0 → No

subtotal.description: This is the total of the line items before discount, before tax and before copays

discountAmount.description:
The amount of the discount applied to the subtotal (pre-tax).
      If the discount was entered in terms of percentage, this is the computed amount.
      If the discount was entered as a fixed dollar amount, it will appear hear.

taxableSubtotal.description: subtotal - discountAmount. This is the amount against which the taxes will be calculated.

taxRate.description: The combined tax rate of state, county and state taxes

taxAmount.description: taxableSubtotal * taxRate/100. This is the $ amount of taxes to be collected

paidAmount.description: Total amount paid for the direct install

payments.description: Array of objects with payment details.

isPaid.description: The line item has been paid

uuid.description: This is a universally unique ID for this item (UUID).

category.description:
Field options:  
installedMeasures → Installed Measures  
other → Other Costs & Adjustments

costPerUnit.description: This is the cost for a single measure. If there is a separate labor cost defined for this line item, then this value (costPerUnit) is understood to be the material cost excluding labor. The complete unit cost of a line item is always: costPerUnit + laborCostPerUnit.

laborCostPerUnit.description: Cost for the labor / installation of a single measure.

unit.description: This is the unit that the quantiy refers to.

lifeInMonths.description: This is the life of the measure expressed in months.

sir.description: This is calculated based on cost and measure life.

fuelType.description:
Field options:  
Natural Gas → Natural Gas  
Electricity → Electricity  
Fuel Oil → Fuel Oil  
Propane → Propane  
Wood → Wood  
Pellets → Pellets  
Solar → Solar  
Don't Know → Don't Know

Choose _Internal_ to keep tickets private to program staff. Choose _External_ to share tickets with trade allies.  
Field options:  
internal → Internal  
external → External

description.description: This is an opportunity to provide details and instructions for this ticket. Providing steps and linking to supporting documents helps people understand what is expected of them.

version.description: Ticket version makes a difference between tickets created from the same template but different versions

type.description: It defines tickets relation to a job's company or program

uuid.description: This is a universally unique ID for this item (UUID).

status.description:
**Recommend** a measure to feature it in the report's upgrade details section. This measure will factor into the job's total cost, savings and SIR.

**Note** a measure to list it in the report as 'Additional Notes'. Noted measures do not count towards the job's total cost, savings and SIR.

**Decline** a measure to hide it altogether report. Declined measures do not count towards the job's total cost, savings and SIR.

  
Field options:  
0 → Default System Recommend  
1 → Recommend  
2 → Note  
3 → Decline

title.description: The title will appear throughout the audit report. When appropriate, consider wording this as an actionable sentence. For instance, instead of 'Programmable Thermostat', you could write 'Program your thermostat'.

measureCode.description: Used to identify a measure with a custom code.

homeownerNotes.description: Add a note to the homeowner about this recommendation

contractorNotes.description: This field is intended to communicate details with other professionals. It can be turned on or off in the report so you can chose to hide technical jargon or other information when presenting to the homeowner.

whyItMatters.description: Best kept short and concise, this field is largely educational and appears in the sidebar of the recommendation.

costOfSelectedLineItemsForIncentive.description: If recommendation costType is 1 (Detailed) "costOfSelectedLineItemsForIncentive" returns the total cost of selected line items for the incentive. It is null for recommendations with costType equals 0 (Simple).

selectedLineItems.description: Array of selected line items for the incentive. It is null for recommendations with costType equals 0 (Simple).

title.example: Baseload is too low relative to bills

summary.example: The most common causes for this errors are: Erroneous pool pump, hot tub spec, incorrect DHW type or DHW efficiency.
